data.title: Top 5 Stocks For AI's Optical Revolution In 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As March closed to an end last week, Nvidia (NVDA) announced another $2B investment into the broader optical connectivity space. This time, Marvell (MRVL) was the benefactor of Nvidia’s continued spree of investing billions into the optical networking industry.

That was Nvidia’s third announcement of the month regarding its investments into securing commitments and forming partnerships in the optical connectivity ecosystem. In just March alone, Nvidia had invested $6B of its cash in companies that had something to do with lasers, optical connectivity solutions, and/or photonics.

Nvidia’s investments in this space are just one way of reflecting upon just how important the optical bottleneck is, underscoring the growing demand for laser chips, optical components, and networking solutions.

In this post I discuss 5 key beneficiaries of the optical industry that I am bullish on and believe sit at the optical bottlenecks. These 5 beneficiaries are Marvell, Lumentum (LITE), Coherent (COHR), Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI), and Ciena (CIEN).

Understanding Nvidia’s Big Bet On Optics

As of Nvidia’s 13F quarterly filings in February, the company had invested ~$15.8B of its cash across 5 companies that were operating in 4 different sectors of the technology industry—CPUs, Neoclouds, EDA (Electronic Design Automation), and AI-RAN (artificial intelligence-radio access network).

Then in March alone, Nvidia splashed $6B across three companies in the optical industry—Lumentum, Coherent, and most recently Marvell. Assuming that Nvidia does not liquidate any of its prior positions from February’s 13F filings, here is how these investments now look for Nvidia.

These investments were a strong follow-up to Nvidia’s GTC26 flagship keynote event, which showed just how important optical connections will become in Nvidia’s proprietary scale-up fabric, NVLink. This was one of the key takeaways from GTC26, where Nvidia has begun to architect a technical adoption away from only copper scale-up interconnects towards the use of optical interconnects as well. I highly recommend reading The Register’s take on the pivotal importance optical connections imply for Nvidia in scale-up. Research indicates that the entire optical industry, including CPO (co-packaged optics) and photonics solutions like optical transceivers, will be likely growing at a minimum 35-40% CAGR over the next two years, with a majority of that growth seen this year.

The path forward in scale-up networks essentially implies laser chips will have a massive part to play in scale-up networks due to the coming wave of optical solutions adoption along with optical pluggables and transceivers that are part of this wave of adoption. Each of these 5 beneficiaries has delivered strong alpha so far this year, at a time when headwinds like geopolitical tensions and trade volatility are capturing the market’s attention.

It does look like a rising tide/wave lifting all players in the optical space, as in the extremely strong performance of players in this space, massively outperforming the S&P 500 index (SP500). And last month’s OFC event further shows how much more demand there is for optical technology solutions.

Here are the 5 companies I am bullish on within the optical connectivity space.

1) Marvell Technology

Let me start with Nvidia’s latest $2B investment, which was with Marvell last week. I recently discussed what the investment means for Marvell.

But my view is very similar to The Register—that Nvidia will use Marvell’s technology to seamlessly integrate XPUs onto Nvidia’s NVLink scale-up fabric. As I had explained in last week’s post, Nvidia is opening up its NVLink platform via the NVLink Fusion fabric to allow for XPUs or third-party CPUs to be integrated onto the NVLink platform, and Marvell’s interconnect solutions can help in “attaching” these XPUs/CPUs onto the platform.

Marvell’s management themselves see their TAM footprint expanding by 26% to $94B due to this renewed focus on XPU attach. Management recently raised their guidance to expect $15B in revenues by FY28, implying ~35% and scope for margins to expand.

Against this backdrop, Marvell trades at 8.8x FY27 projected sales of ~$11B, up ~34% while also trading at a P/E of 28x. The forward PEG ratio of just 0.7 shows how undervalued Marvell is relative to its expected FY27 earnings growth.

2 & 3) Lumentum Holdings & Coherent Corp.

The reason I decided to club these stocks together is because the end component these companies make is very similar—laser chips. Except it's the underlying laser technology of these chips that creates a divergence in their revenue growth, as I explained in an earlier post.

Note: Nvidia has invested $2B each in Lumentum and in Coherent.

Lumentum’s EML chips (electro-absorption modulated) are already widely used in CPO-based switches like the Quantum-X800 Photonics and the Spectrum-X800 Photonics switches, while Coherent’s CW laser chips (continuous wave) are expected to be deployed in GPUs I noted in my post on Lumentum and Coherent. This was also strongly hinted at by Coherent’s management in their Q2 FY26 call, where they said that Coherent won “an exceptionally large purchase order” from a leading AI customer for CPO solutions that will integrate Coherent’s CW lasers and that this order would be meaningfully accretive in FY27.

That’s why Coherent has already started significantly ramping up their capacity of InP (indium phosphide), a critical raw material used in manufacturing laser chips. At the OFC event, Coherent revealed that they will 2x their InP capacity in FY26 with another 2x increase in InP capacity planned for FY27. This significantly changes Coherent’s ability to serve the demand for laser chips in the optical solutions market for data centers and uniquely distinguishes Coherent from Lumentum, something I believe is not priced in today yet. That’s because Coherent follows a vertically integrated model of manufacturing all the core raw material for their laser chips ground up, while Lumentum sourced its InP capacity from third-party vendors.

At the moment, Coherent’s revenues are projected to grow ~20% in FY26, then accelerate to 26% in FY27, while Lumentum’s revenues are growing at ~3x the pace of Coherent’s top line, i.e., 77% growth in FY26 marginally slowing to 69% in FY27.

Coherent trades at ~7.7x forward sales and 48x forward earnings. This is still 2-3x cheaper as compared to Lumentum’s 21x forward sales and 107x forward earnings. But since Lumentum is growing its business at 3x, as I mentioned in the earlier paragraph, Lumentum’s valuation may be warranted. I continue to like both these stocks here at current levels.

4) Ciena Corp.

Unlike Marvell, Lumentum, or Coherent, Ciena is not listed as a strategic business partner to Nvidia but sells products to Nvidia’s hyperscaler customers.

A majority of Ciena’s orders in the data center market have been for the company’s pluggable transceiver modules so far. That has led to significant acceleration in Ciena’s revenue base with ~19% growth in FY25 vs. revenue contraction in the previous year.

While management had projected $5.9-6.3B in FY26 revenues, implying 24-32% growth, analysts are estimating Ciena to deliver $6.12B, 28% growth, implying meaningful bullish projections for Ciena. One of the reasons for that, as I noted last month, was the rapid growth in Ciena’s backlog and its ability to service that backlog. Ciena’s backlog is growing at a 45-46% pace to ~$7B.

Moving forward, the company expects its Vesta 200 6.4T CPX, a CPO-based optical solution targeted at hyperscalers, to be a strong growth vector for Ciena, in addition to the transceiver modules.

Ciena’s valuation premiums are probably the richest of the lot, in my opinion, as compared to its forward growth outlook. The company trades at 10x forward sales and 73x forward earnings, but a PEG ratio of 1.1 implies some level of attractive positioning in Ciena’s earnings multiple vs. forward earnings expected to grow 132% this year.

5) Applied Optoelectronics

Like Ciena, Applied Optoelectronics is also not an official strategic business partner to Nvidia but sells to Nvidia’s hyperscaler customers.

In my view, Applied Optoelectronics, or AOI, is a kind of dark horse among these 5 beneficiaries of the optical revolution that I have so far stated in this post.

On one hand are the customer concentration and prior execution risks that I had explained in depth last month. On the other hand, there is the possibility that management may have learned from its prior mistakes and is sufficiently demonstrating robust capex expansion to build enough production capacity so that it can deliver product shipments on/before time to its key hyperscaler customers.

This is what I noticed in my most recent assessment on AOI, where new hyperscaler customers like Amazon (AMZN) are ramping up their purchases of AOI’s optical transceivers. Like Coherent, AOI is also a vertically integrated company that produces its own laser supply and manufactures its transceiver modules.

Similar to many companies that I highlighted in this post, AOI trades at a forward sales multiple of ~8.4x. But, in my opinion, AOI’s sales multiple of 8.4x is quite attractive given that management projects sales to grow 119% to $1B this year. A majority of its growth this year is going to come from its data center sales segment, which management expects will jump 3.6x to $700M in sales this year. AOI’s forward sales multiples are too cheap to ignore at these levels, despite the risk of execution and customer concentration in the background.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

So far, these stocks have bucked the slump seen in overall markets because of the sustained background narrative of AI capex continuing to be deployed towards building out data centers. Given this narrative of AI capex, the growing adoption of optical solutions and components provided by these beneficiary companies stated in this article that sit at the bottleneck optical networks will continue to be advantageous to all 5 companies.

However, if AI capex were to suddenly stop getting deployed or if the global economic outlook takes a turn for the worse, these stocks will get hurt, partly because many names here depend on AI capex from the top 4-5 hyperscalers in the world.

Takeaway

Similar to how Nvidia has altered their portfolio to include exposure to optical connectivity stocks, my portfolio has also been adjusted to reflect similar beneficiaries.

I am bullish on all five names that I have listed in this post, three of which Nvidia has already invested in.

data.title: KeyCorp Turns The Key On Loan Growth And An Increased Stake From Scotiabank
data.description:
While it may not always get the media headlines of the big global banks, there is one regional player that has been on my watchlist for a few years as one of my best-performing buy ratings so far.

KeyCorp (KEY), the parent behind KeyBank, is up around +114% since I gave it a strong buy in July 2023 and also up since my subsequent hold and buy ratings in 2024 and 2025.

Some "key" points that I loved about KeyCorp were their expansion beyond their local roots, their investment-grade ratings, and a compelling dividend story.

The bank, which actually has roots going back to the 1800s, according to its Seeking Alpha profile, and has a consumer and commercial segment, was actually in major financial news a few weeks ago when Canada's Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS) is "seeking to increase its ownership in KeyCorp to as much as 19.99%, up from its current stake of 14.99%."

Against the backdrop of that positive news, today's update is also a preview of this stock ahead of its upcoming Apr. 16th earnings results.

Thesis Summary

Based on my updated research, and ahead of its upcoming earnings call this month, I'm reiterating my prior buy rating on this stock.

Although I realize the current macro environment is full of headwinds, and some banks have proven to have stronger historical performance than this one, its upside can be driven by loan growth and improving margins, along with a low balance sheet risk profile, a compelling dividend growth story, and an undervaluation opportunity coupled with future upside expected.

The above rating worksheet shows how my 8 different rating categories across fundamental and technical analysis drove the average rating score. Keep reading to find out what I thought of each topic in detail.

Macro & Sector Trends

When it comes to the macroeconomic and sector environment, I gave this section a hold rating, and here is why.

To help understand the type of banking niche KeyCorp is in, I considered its 4Q25 investor deck, which highlights a considerable loan business (both consumer and commercial) but also a fees-driven business focusing on areas like mortgage servicing, trust services, some investment banking activity, and others.

Some financial sector thought leaders have forecasted a positive outlook for regional banks this year. Consider what Angel Oak Capital Advisors wrote in a note in February:

Large-cap banks have led performance recently, but the next 18–24 months could mark the turning point for regional banks. With cleaner balance sheets, improving margins, and valuations near decade lows, the risk-reward is compelling.

While this painted a rosy picture back in February, since then the economy has been hit with a major potential headwind with the geopolitical crisis in the Middle East and the resulting oil shock that emerged, which as of this writing on Monday has not yet resolved. This begs the question, what impact could it have on a major upside driver for a regional bank, loan demand.

On April 1st, for instance, CNBC's Diana Olick wrote:

Mortgage rates moved even higher again last week, as the war with Iran continues to stoke fears of inflation. As a result, total mortgage application volume fell again, down 10.4% from the previous week, according to the Mortgage Bankers Association’s seasonally adjusted index.

However, what could challenge a negative thesis could be a quick end to the global conflicts and price stability in the energy market, which I think could reinvigorate the demand for business borrowing for growth.

We already saw tailwinds in demand by mid-size enterprises in Q4, as Reuters reported back in February:

Business loan demand from large and medium-sized firms in the fourth quarter was the strongest it has been since the second quarter of 2022, the Fed's quarterly Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey found.

Hence, I am on the fence when it comes to the macro environment, in a watch-and-wait mode.

Competitive Positioning

Moving on, I ran a competitive positioning comparison and decided to give this category a hold rating, after considering both quantitative and qualitative factors.

The peer comparables I picked were a sampling of regional US banks in the market cap range of $20B - $50B, and that have a mix of consumer and commercial banking. This peer group should be differentiated from large global banks like JP Morgan Chase (JPM) and even Bank of America (BAC), which have a much bigger global footprint along with significant penetration of trading/investing and wealth advisory.

So, the comps I picked were: Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB), Citizens Financial (CFG), Huntington Bancshares (HBAN).

From peer data, KeyCorp found itself on the bottom of the pile for historic top-line growth, with a 5-year revenue CAGR of just 4.3%, while coming in 3rd in this group for return on equity, with a RoCE of 10%.

In addition, although it saw a positive 10-year price return of 76.5%, which is respectable, momentum data also shows that it not only was far from the performance of the S&P500 but also behind Fifth Third and Citizens, indicating that the market have had historically had weaker momentum for KeyCorp.

While those quantitative factors above put KeyCorp in a neutral range in this peer group, when considering qualitative factors, I would highlight their bank brand's considerably vast branch network across many US states, as seen on the company website, which says they are in 15 states and in 950 full-service branches.

I think this geographic diversity and penetration could certainly help in competitiveness, particularly in consumer banking, which is very driven by recognizable and trusted brands. In fact, banking is all about trust.

So, this further strengthens a neutral view for this stock, rather than a bearish one.

Company Growth Factors

In considering what I think could impact company growth in the near-term, I gave this stock a buy rating in this section.

Based on having researched many bank stocks on this platform in recent years, I found that some key growth factors for a bank could come from acquisition, loan growth, and also net new client inflows.

On the acquisition front, KeyCorp already ruled that out in the near-term it seems, with Reuters reporting in December that the bank "is not looking to pursue acquisitions despite consolidation in the industry and will use its excess capital for share buybacks."

Also, since KeyCorp does not seem to have a very huge wealth advisory or asset management segment like some bigger banking names, what I would point to as a potential growth driver is the YoY growth in commercial loans, which I think can drive future upside via future interest income.

Consider that from the 4Q25 investor deck (pg 7), the commercial segment helped drive up average loans overall:

So, a major item I'll be looking for in the upcoming earnings results this April is a continuation of this trend, particularly since it seems like the consumer lending trend has slowed down for them. In their Q4 deck, they attributed some of that to "intentional run-off of low yielding consumer mortgages."

The fees-driven business also saw some YoY growth momentum in Q4, with the Q4 press release (pg 3) saying it was in part driven by "a $22MM increase in investment banking and debt placement fees, a $12MM increase in corporate services income, and continued momentum in trust and investment services and commercial payments."

So, Key has shown you don't necessarily have to be a Morgan Stanley (MS) to grow fee income, and I think this adds some nice diversification to the bank beyond just interest income, which, of course, is impacted by interest rate risk.

Complementing this positive direction is Seeking Alpha data showing expected fwd revenue growth at around 23%, easily beating the sector average. I think if Key can continue executing on top line improvement, it could turn around some of those weaker revenue CAGR metrics I showed earlier vs. peers.

Nevertheless, I am bullish in this section.

Earnings Outlook

In this part, I considered some impacts to earnings, and my main focus was the role of margins, which seem to support a buy rating in this section.

A key metric I follow is the profit margin vs. peers, and from the data below we can see KeyCorp at +16.3%, putting it within the lower/middle part of this group, but it also shows from the dark blue trend line that its margin has significantly improved over the 1 year period shown, and that is great news:

Again, the next earnings call is soon, and I'll be looking for a continuation of this trend.

A key cost for a bank is interest expense, since some major ways it funds itself are via depositors but also borrowing, both of which could drive interest costs. So, a key metric in banking is the net interest margin, and since I like to point out patterns and trends, we can see from the Q4 investor deck (pg 9) that KeyCorp has achieved a proven, steady improvement in its net interest margin:

I think this may have helped to boost analyst confidence, since the consensus has 6 upward earnings revisions on this stock vs. just 4 downward (data updated as of Wednesday morning), and consensus estimates expect 17 - 21% YoY EPS growth this year and next.

The core business also has seen a very nice operating margin recovery in the 3 year period shown below, indicating the business has rebounded.

These favorable metrics above, along with the increased investment from Scotiabank that I mentioned earlier, could support bullishness from the market in the near term, and so that is my view here, a bullish one.

Balance Sheet Risk

Next, I researched some elements of balance sheet risk, and actually gave this stock a strong buy rating in this category, one of its best aspects.

Banks are in particular scrutinized on balance sheet risk, due to their potential systemic risk in the market and economy if the risk needle goes too far in the wrong direction. So, here I considered this bank's credit ratings, debt-to-equity vs. peers, and a look at some asset risk exposure.

Notably, KeyCorp has investment-grade credit ratings from three major agencies as of the most recent available data: BBB from S&P, Baa2 from Moody's, and A- from Fitch.

In fact, last autumn, Fitch upgraded this bank, saying in its rating note that "rebuilt capital and liquidity buffers support KEY's competitive position and capacity to pursue growth."

As for D/E, this entire peer group is below 1.0, and KeyCorp, at 0.56, is not only on the lower end of the group, but its trend line (orange line) is declining in the last year, indicating lower leverage vs. equity.

I should reiterate, I am not against leverage, and I understand its role in driving business growth, but what I do care about is patterns and trends, such as whether this bank is a major outlier vs. its peer group and whether its leverage is unjustifiably high compared to similar regional banks.

On the asset risk side, since a key asset risk is bad loans that customers are struggling to pay off, or default completely, I looked at the trend from the 4Q25 earnings release (pg 5), showing both charge-offs and nonperforming loans decreasing significantly on a YoY basis (4Q25 vs 4Q24):

It will be critical for the bank to continue showing this improvement trend in the upcoming quarterly results again to show that this trend is sustainable and not impacted by any Q1 headwinds. Otherwise, any negative signals here could hurt bullish confidence.

Given the strong credit rating, low D/E, and loan improvement trends, I am actually highly bullish in this section.

Dividend Outlook

Now, moving on to dividends, which is materially relevant since myself and many of my readers are dividend income investors. Here, I gave the stock a buy rating.

In my very early coverage of this stock, I really loved the dividend yield, and still do, and as of Monday it was at around 4%, putting it above the sector average.

Also, the dividend growth case continues to be compelling for me, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +10.5%, also above the sector median, according to dividend growth data on Seeking Alpha.

In fact, growth data on Seeking Alpha shows this bank's resilience and not having cut their dividend through various market shocks like pandemics, interest rate shocks, tariff wars, and inflation. While this may be historic data, it could also point to how the stock may probabilistically behave in future such shocks.

We should also consider the dividend payout ratio is around 55%, so while it may be somewhat elevated compared to some companies, it still ought to leave plenty of buffer between earnings and dividend payouts.

For the combined reasons above, I am bullish in this section.

Valuation Metrics

In the worksheet below, for inputs I used comparison data and consensus estimates, and based on my findings, I am a strong buy in this section.

The stock is relatively in line with peers on price/book value, but slightly undervalued on fwd P/E, which I think could be overly cautious since KeyCorp has shown improving margins, but also has strong analyst EPS forecasts.

So, I see it as an undervaluation opportunity with a potential +17% price upside for a Dec. 2027 target date, justifying a highly bullish and long-term bet on this stock. By comparison, the average Wall Street forecast as of Monday evening also expects around +17% upside, so similar to my own view.

Technical Charting

In this final section, to add an additional perspective, I considered the technical chart below and from the pattern I gave it a hold rating.

The last clear buy signal happened last summer, when the 50-day SMA crossed above the 200-day SMA. Since then, the stock seems to be in both a bullish and neutral trend, but certainly no strong bearish signals like a death cross.

Because the most recent bullish trend seems to have broken, but the stock is also still trading above the long-term moving average, it seems to justify a neutral view right now.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

We have already talked about some geopolitical and energy risks to the US economy, and brand competition from other banks, so one other thing that could challenge my bullish thesis that has not been mentioned is something I noticed in the section on growth.

Its loan growth is lopsided, driven by commercial but hindered by the consumer segment. So, if Q1 results see a spike in consumer loan declines, not just due to runoff of low-yielding consumer mortgages but also from lower loan demand, that could further make the loan growth case lopsided and heavily dependent on business borrowing demand.

Conclusion

Today's article summed up why I am still bullish on KeyCorp and why it remains on my watchlist of regional bank ideas, as well as dividend income ideas for 2026.

Despite some uncertain directions of the larger economy as of this writing and not knowing what energy costs and supplies will do in the near term, I have taken an approach of selective diversification rather than broad-based, and so my call to action for readers is to take another look at some gems like this in the regional banking niche.

data.title: Armanino Foods: Growth Initiatives Are There, Execution Now Matters
data.description:
Investment thesis

Armanino Foods (OTC:AMNF) sells pesto and other sauces directly to restaurants, where quality matters more than name or trending brands. This has allowed it to maintain very high margins, even exceeding the industry average while also growing organically at 8-10%. I believe the prospects for continued high margin growth remain strong and the recent drawdown has provided an opportunity to buy this good business at a reasonable valuation.

Quality as the competitive advantage

Armanino is a very simple business to understand, as is typical of food companies. They basically make frozen Italian food, such as pesto, other sauces, pasta, meatballs and even cheeses. Nothing particularly disruptive, but it has allowed them to grow their sales at a rate of 15% annually since 2021, but I think a reasonable growth rate that we could expect in the future would be around 8-10%, like in the last 3 years if we remove the atypically high growth of fiscal year 2022. Still, it remains an attractive growth rate for a food company.

Armanino's main focus is selling to restaurants through large food distributors like Sysco or US Foods. In my opinion, this approach is more interesting in terms of the quality of sales because the company depend less on branding and more on product, since a restaurant isn't looking for the new trending ingredient on TikTok. Rather, they're looking for reliability. And once you're in, you tend to stay in, because if a kitchen uses Armanino's sauces on its menu, changing it means risking a loss of consistency. We've all experienced a local restaurant losing its signature flavor, which makes you less inclined to go there again. That’s the kind of friction that works in Armanino’s favor.

The company also has a secondary growth opportunity its presence in the retail sector, with its products available in supermarkets. I can't say it's a particularly significant segment and the company doesn't break down its sales by channel, but management believes it represents an additional growth opportunity.

And then you can also see where we are currently selling in the retail channel, and that continues to be an area that we expand and grow as well. Deanna Jurgens - CEO & President

In my opinion, retail can help increase brand awareness among a wider audience, but it loses some of the customer loyalty that comes with being featured on a restaurant menu and also competition is more focused on price, which (as you can imagine) ends up reducing margins.

Growth initiatives

The following image shows how this approach of competing on quality within the foodservice segment, rather than on price in supermarkets, ends up bringing the company better margins than many comparable companies, even those with much more scale like Kraft Heinz (KHC) or McCormick (MKC).

One area where I think the company fell short in Q4 2025 was top-line growth, reporting only 5.5% growth. Considering that the stock traded at demanding multiples of over 20 times earnings at the beginning of the year, this could explain the 16% drop in the stock price since January. However, operating income also increased by 20% in that quarter, so it wasn't all bad news.

But honestly, I don't think this quarter marks the end of the company's growth. Management has been very clear about where they expect growth to come from. The first strategy involves getting more value from existing customers by selling them additional sauces like Southwest Chipotle or Chimichurri, which allows them to reach restaurants that don't use pesto on their menu or that aren't necessarily focused on Italian food.

The international expansion initiative, with Asia as the initial target, is also interesting. This is a region where the company already had active businesses, but had never focused on developing them further, so this helps confirm that a market exists there and reduces the risk of uncertainty about its viability.

Valuation

My baseline estimate assumes there won't be explosive growth in the coming years and the 8-10% growth I mentioned earlier will continue. Also, I believe margins can be maintained at current levels if the growth initiatives in the foodservice segment are sustained. Therefore, with these assumptions, the company would be earning around $0.90-$0.95 per share within five years, which, with a reasonable earnings multiple of 18 times earnings for the sector and its peers, represents a share price of almost $17.

However, there's another thing to consider: the company has approximately $28.9 million in net cash (after deducting total debt from current cash on the balance sheet). If we include this cash, the share price would be around $18.

This doesn't include the current dividend of $0.05 per share quarterly, which would represent a yield of almost 2% if annualized. It should also be noted that the company authorized a $12 million share buyback program, of which $7.5 million had been executed as of Q3 2025, which would improve EPS by reducing shares outstanding.

Risks

The first risk that particularly concerns me is the concentration of customers, especially on the food distributor side. At the close of fiscal year 2025, there was a single distributor accounting for 53% of sales and another in Asia accounting for 8%. One could argue that demand comes from end consumers and that distributors are somewhat interchangeable, but the loss of a key distributor would almost certainly cause problems, at least in the short term.

I also think that since the business is tied to restaurants, any economic slowdown could directly affect demand, and therefore restaurants might place lower orders volume. While it's not exactly a discretionary consumer product, I do believe a weak economy could impact sales or even profit margins.

Also, as a micro-cap OTC stock, liquidity is also limited, which may not suit all investors. It was worth mentioning that.

The bottom line

While the recent quarter was somewhat weak, I don't believe the company's growth is over. The initiatives are there, the product has shown some traction and I think Armanino developed the competitive advantages to continue its growth and hold its margins. Of course, it's not a risk-free investment, but at the same time, I believe that the current valuation of 18x earnings is already a reasonable starting point for a long-term position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Ceasefire With A Bitter Aftertaste For The U.S.
data.description:
Last night, we went through a news rollercoaster. In a couple of hours we went from threats that “Iran’s civilization will die” to an unexpected two-week ceasefire. The markets are celebrating this news with one of the biggest upside in a while; while I am writing, S&P 500 futures (SPX) are up almost by 3% and WTI oil (CL1:COM) is plunging by 15%.

Are these the early signs that the war is about to end? Well, in my opinion I have multiple doubts, both on the potential end of the war and on the underlying meaning behind this ceasefire. The market wants to believe that this war has an easy resolution, a bit like what happened with Liberation Day. However, in this case we have many more variables that make me believe that we still need to act cautiously: it is not yet the time to go all in and be fully invested.

The ceasefire is skewed in Iran's favor

President Trump declared 100% victory after U.S.-Iran ceasefire and the markets are celebrating. But is this a rational reaction? At first glance, I was surprised by this ceasefire, in fact, Iranian leaders have stated multiple times that they would have not accepted a temporary halt of the war: either it goes on or it ends forever. So, did they change their mind? Well, not exactly.

The ceasefire agreed yesterday is based on Iran’s 10-point proposal, which is now considered the basis for negotiations over the next two weeks. The problem is that, obviously, Iran’s 10-point proposal is totally skewed in its favor. Just to mention a few:

  • Ending the war in the whole region, including Israeli attacks toward Lebanon.
  • Withdrawal of U.S. combat forces from the Middle East.
  • Control over the Strait of Hormuz together with Oman. Even though not explicitly stated, it is likely included the $2 million toll on every ship crossing the Strait. Before the war broke out, more than 100 ships crossed the Strait every day, so Iran could earn at least $100 million per day in the future.
  • Release of Iranian assets blocked abroad, on top of removing the primary and secondary sanctions.
  • No more wars between U.S. and Iran in the future, but U.S. must accept Iran’s uranium enrichment (not enriched enough to create nuclear weapons).
  • Receive payments for the damages caused by the war.

Of course, Iran will never get everything it asks, but this is now the basis for negotiations. What about U.S., what does it get? Well, it is hard to say. In these points there is no mention of regime change, withdrawal from the ballistic missile program and not even willingness to not enrich uranium anymore. Iran has only promised to not create nuclear weapons, something that was already achieved a decade ago by the Obama administration.

In other words, from a diplomatic perspective, after 40 days of war, the U.S. hasn’t reached a better position than in the past, quite the contrary. Iran has more influence over the Strait of Hormuz, and with ship tolls and sanctions relief, it will quickly repair the war damages.

On the other hand, I don’t see how the U.S. economy has benefited from this war, assuming that we are now entering a phase of de-escalation. Sure, making a step toward the end of the war is positive for the economy and reduces the probabilities of a recession (which is why the stock market is soaring), but the U.S. are leaving this war only after the global supply chain has been destroyed for 40 days. This is not Liberation Day where you lift and reduce tariffs in a couple of days without severe consequences; the Iran-U.S. war has destroyed strategic infrastructures that can’t operate at full capacity anytime soon:

  • According to QuatarEnergy CEO, Iranian attacks in mid-March wiped out 17% Qatar’s LNG capacity for up to five years.
  • Ruwais refining complex in the United Arab Emirates is one of the most important in the world and has been hit multiple times during the war.
  • Kuwait National Petroleum Co. and Petrochemical Industries Co. facilities have been hit hard very recently, on April 5.

These are just few examples, and prove that even if the war ends today once and for all, the energy supply chain won’t be as efficient as before the war broke out. It needs time, so I don’t expect the price of oil to get back to early February levels over the next weeks. Sure, it is now crashing 15% following the Iranian announcement that it will open up the Strait of Hormuz during the two-week ceasefire, but it is still 40% higher than 40 days ago.

Furthermore, this is not the first time it crashes so hard. In mid-March it dropped by almost 35% in two trading days just to bounce back to $117 per barrel over the next weeks. When you are in middle of an energy crisis, oil tends to be extremely volatile and gives a lot of false hopes. Take a look at what happened in 2022.

In about 6 months, it took 9 double-digit crashes for the WTI Crude oil price not to be traded above the $100 per barrel threshold. Therefore, a 15% drop in oil price is totally normal during energy crisis.

Finally, the markets are celebrating the opening of the Strait of Hormuz. Thousands of ships that have been blocked near the Coast can finally cross the Strait and deliver their products across the world. So, there will be a long line at Hormuz, right? Well, not exactly.

While I am writing, the ceasefire agreement occurred 11 hours ago, but the Strait of Hormuz is still almost empty. What is preventing oil tankers from crossing it? Charlie Brown, a former U.S. Navy veteran gives us the answer:

The ceasefire is a necessary first step, but it does not mean commercial shipping immediately normalizes through the international traffic lanes in the Strait.

Shipowners are still waiting for authoritative guidance from naval security channels, flag states, and, critically, marine war-risk insurers before sending vessels back into the strait.

The real signal to watch is the ‘first movers’ –– the earliest vessels willing to test the route. If those transits are completed safely, confidence will build quickly and the broader watch-and-wait cohort will follow.

His vision is supported by a recent statement of Maersk (AMKBY), one of the world’s biggest container shipping groups:

At this point, we take a cautious approach, and we are not making any changes to specific services. Any decision to transit the Strait of Hormuz will be based on continuous risk assessments, close monitoring of the security situation, and available guidance from relevant authorities and partners.

All in all, the ceasefire should last 2 weeks, but ships are not crossing yet the Strait. I don’t know how long it will take to mobilize them, but it doesn’t bode well on this first day of ceasefire.

The situation in Lebanon is still unclear

One of the most important points in the Iran’s 10-point proposal is the end of every conflict in the region, including the Israeli attacks against Lebanon. However, Netanyahu has clearly affirmed that the U.S. – Iran ceasefire doesn’t include Lebanon.

Hezbollah, the militant group in Lebanon, was initially willing to accept the ceasefire but it is changing opinion since Israel has never stopped bombing.

Ibrahim Moussawi, an Hezbollah member of parliament is pressing Israel to immediately stop the attacks, otherwise the U.S.-Iranian ceasefire deal could collapse altogether. The market is totally downplaying this risk but it is real.

Final thoughts

Fortunately, last night we had no civilization destruction and the peace talks prevailed. Today, our investment portfolios are getting some fresh air, but I still have many doubts about the following weeks. U.S. seems prone to start negotiations based on Iran’s 10-point proposal, which is something that I couldn’t believe possible a couple of days.

I think it is likely that the final agreement will be skewed in Iran’s favor, but apparently the market doesn’t care. The only thing that matters is opening the Strait, and Iran did it for 2 weeks. However, ships still don’t cross it and Israel might jeopardize the ceasefire due to ongoing attacks on Lebanon (they have just carried out one of the most powerful attacks since March 2) . By the way, and that’s a breaking news as I write, Iran’s Lavan oil refinery just got hit despite the U.S. ceasefire. This further fuels my doubts.

Oil prices are crashing but relevant energy infrastructures won’t get back at full capacity. They need time, and this means systematically higher energy prices for the time being. In addition to energy, fertilizer prices surged as well and are threatening farmers’ profits. Food and energy have typically an inelastic demand, and we are going to see the full effect of this war only with March-April CPI releases.

Overall, the ceasefire is definitely a positive news, but celebrating the start of a new bull market might be too soon. There are still lots of variables that might complicate the situation, and we are still in a technical downtrend.

Energy crises typically don’t last 40 days, and reaching a final agreement might take longer than 2 weeks. This could be the first of multiple ceasefires, especially if Israel doesn’t follow U.S. guidelines.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ETO: Expect Volatility If Inflation Surges This Summer
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund (ETO) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its shareholders with a high level of total return. However, the fund might also be attractive to income-seeking investors as it boasts an attractive 7.63% yield at the current share price. This yield is substantially higher than that of nearly any equity index and even most fixed-income indices, so it might work reasonably well for anyone who is trying to use their investment portfolio as a source of income. In addition to this, the fund’s portfolio is much more heavily weighted towards equities than fixed-income, so the fund should be able to protect its shareholders against any decline in purchasing power that accompanies inflation. As regular readers are likely aware, I am quite concerned that inflation going forward will be much higher than what most of us have become accustomed to in our daily lives, so inflation protection tends to be something that I usually seek out when purchasing assets. However, we should note that this fund is rather heavily weighted towards the technology sector just like most other Eaton Vance closed-end funds. Investors who already have a lot of technology exposure should keep that in mind before deciding to initiate or add to a position in this fund.

About The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund

The website for the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund states that:

The Fund invests primarily in global dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and seeks to distribute a high level of dividend income that qualifies for favorable federal income tax treatment.

This description explicitly states that ETO invests in common equities and preferred equities. However, the fund actually has a sizable allocation to corporate bonds. The most recent portfolio holdings report is dated January 31, 2026. This document states that the fund had the following asset allocation as of that date:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Common Stocks

100.9%

Corporate Bonds

16.2%

Preferred Stocks

1.4%

Miscellaneous

0.0%

Money Market Fund

0.3%

The fund’s holdings report shows that the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund does not have the asset allocation that we would expect it to have based on the website’s description. The description states that the fund invests in common stocks and preferred stocks, and it says nothing about bonds. However, the fund’s actual holdings have a much higher allocation to bonds than to preferred stocks. This will impact the fund’s performance and potentially the way that its distributions are taxed.

Bonds and preferred stocks are both considered to be fixed-income securities, and in practice, both do have some things in common. For example, the coupon payments made by traditional bonds and the dividend payments made by preferred stocks are typically fixed at the time that the securities are first issued. Unlike common stock dividends, the payments made by bonds and preferred stocks have no relationship with the financial performance of the underlying company. This means that the income that they provide will not increase over time. That could be a problem during periods in which inflation is present, as the purchasing power of the income that these securities provide to their owners gradually decreases over time.

In addition to this, most preferred stocks are perpetual securities that do not have maturity dates (or if they do, it is so far into the future that it may as well not have one). This differs from bonds, as bonds always have a maturity date that specifies when owners of the bond receive the face value of the bond back. In practice, this means that a bond will normally have a shorter duration than a preferred stock with an identical yield. Investopedia defines duration thusly:

Duration measures how long it takes in years for an investor to be repaid a bond’s price through its total cash flows.

While Investopedia specifically mentions bonds, common stocks and preferred stocks also have duration, and it means the same thing. In the case of stocks, duration refers to the amount of time that it takes for the dividends to fully cover the investor’s purchase price of the stock. Common stocks issued by very low-dividend-paying companies (such as those in the technology sector) have much higher duration than common stocks issued by companies in high-yielding sectors such as energy. By the same token, preferred stocks usually have a lower duration than common stocks issued by the same company because preferreds usually have higher yields (which means that the dividends paid by the preferred stocks more quickly cover the purchase price of the security).

Duration is also a measure of how sensitive the security is to changes in interest rates. A low-duration security will hold its price better than a high-duration security will whenever interest rates rise and vice versa. As the payments that both bonds and preferred stocks make to their owners are fixed at issuance, this should suggest that a preferred stock will experience a greater price change than a bond with an identical yield will in response to changing interest rates. We saw this during the interest rate hiking cycle a few years ago:

This chart shows the price of American preferred stocks (PFF) graphed against American investment-grade bonds (AGG) and American junk bonds (JNK). The period shown is November 1, 2021, through July 31, 2023. As you may recall, market interest rates first started to rise in response to the inflationary surge following the COVID-19 pandemic in November of 2021, a few months before the Federal Reserve first started hiking interest rates. The Federal Reserve’s final rate hike was in July of 2023, so stopping the chart at the end of that month seemed reasonable. Overall, this chart shows that preferred stocks are generally more sensitive to interest rate changes than bonds are, which fits with the above discussion regarding duration.

Another important thing to note is that shorter-duration securities are usually better at maintaining an investor’s purchasing power in the face of inflation than longer-duration securities. This does not necessarily hold true with common equities, but it is true for fixed-income securities. The reason is that the more rapidly an investor gets their money back, the less time that inflation has to compound against them. This is why I have, in the past, recommended that investors who actually want fixed-income exposure at all stick to short-duration bonds. As I expect inflation to be a permanent fixture of our lives going forward (at least for individuals who use U.S. dollars as their primary currency), short-duration bonds will do a better job of maintaining the purchasing power of our wealth than long-term low-yielding bonds. In this respect, then, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is making a pretty good choice favoring bonds over preferred stocks. After all, bonds have lower durations than preferred stocks with the same yield. Of course, if the fund’s bond holdings consist primarily of long-term U.S. Treasuries or low-yielding investment-grade corporate bonds then this might not be the case.

Unfortunately, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund does not provide a breakdown of its bond holdings by credit rating on either its website or in its fact sheet. As such, we cannot determine at a glance whether the bonds in the fund’s portfolio are investment-grade bonds or junk bonds. The fund does explicitly state that they are not U.S. Treasuries. We could try to make a guess by looking at the yields of the bonds, though. Here is a selection of some of the bonds in the fund’s portfolio:

We see yields ranging from 4.00% to as high as 10%, although most of the bonds shown here are less than 7%. This suggests that most of these bonds are investment-grade corporates, although a handful might be BB or B-rated junk debt. As we can see here, anything rated lower had coupon yields of higher than 10% for nearly all of the past ten years:

Thus, the fund might be sacrificing some yield by holding bonds instead of preferred stocks. Admittedly, though, it might not be a huge sacrifice given that the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), which tracks the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index, has had a yield of 5% to 8% or so for as long as I can remember (and I first started watching it around the time of the subprime mortgage crisis and even bought shares of it in order to profit from a recovery in banks). Seeking Alpha provides this chart showing the yield history of PFF:

So, based on this, the fund might not be sacrificing much income by favoring bonds over preferred stocks, given the coupon yields of the bonds shown in its most recent schedule of investments.

As mentioned earlier, there is one problem with the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund choosing to favor bonds over preferred stocks, however, and it relates to the way that the securities are taxed. As anyone who has ever included bonds in their portfolio knows, the coupon payments that bonds pay to their owners are taxed at ordinary income rates. This is the reason why most financial planners recommend that an investor put bonds into a tax-advantaged account (such as an IRA) rather than an ordinary taxable account. Preferred stock dividends are taxed differently. Many preferred stock dividends are taxed the same way that common stock dividends are (preferred stock dividends are qualified dividends and could be taxed at the long-term capital gains rate in certain circumstances), which means that investors in the higher tax brackets pay less in tax when receiving dividends from preferred stocks than they pay when receiving coupons from a bond. The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is a passthrough entity, so its shareholders have to pay the same tax rate on the distributions that they would have had to pay if they held all of the fund’s assets directly. Thus, the fact that the fund is favoring bonds instead of preferred stocks might expose its shareholders to a higher level of taxation than would be the case without the bonds. The website states that the fund’s distributions consist entirely of net investment income:

However, the annual report states that the fund does not have sufficient net investment income to fully cover the distribution. The tax guide that Eaton Vance released for this fund states that 53.31% of the fund’s distributions for 2025 were ordinary income, but 100% of this amount could be considered qualified for the lower long-term capital gains tax rate in certain circumstances. Thus, it might be okay to hold this fund in a standard taxable brokerage account despite the bond allocation.

Common Stock Portfolio And Commentary

As we just saw, the bulk of the assets of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund are invested in common stocks. As of January 31, 2026, the fund’s common stock portfolio was valued at 100.9% of the fund’s net assets. As of December 31, 2025, the fund held common equities valued at 84.49% of total assets:

It is unclear when the fund’s website has data that is a month older than the portfolio data found in its most recent N-PORT filing. Certainly, it would be a simple matter to update the website whenever the fund files its holdings report with the Securities and Exchange Commission. For some reason, the fund did not do this. The N-PORT filing states that the fund’s common stock portfolio was valued at $544,110,866 as of January 31, 2026. The fund’s total assets were $640,345,498 as of the same date, so its common stock portfolio was 84.97% on January 31, 2026, which is slightly higher than the figure on the website. It is close enough to the website’s figures that we probably do not need to alter the way that we look at this fund. It is primarily an equity fund with a tranche allocated to fixed-income securities to provide it with a higher level of income.

The largest holdings in the fund’s portfolio consist mostly of a mixture of technology, pharmaceuticals, and banks:

The one thing that immediately jumps out is how much exposure this fund has to the information technology sector. Alphabet (GOOG), NVIDIA (NVDA), Micron Technology (MU), Amazon.com (AMZN), ASML Holding (ASML), and Apple (AAPL) combined account for 20.39% of the portfolio. This is, admittedly, lower than the weighting that the S&P 500 Index (SP500) has to the information technology sector, but it is still a lot of exposure. This is not really unusual for an Eaton Vance fund, however, as most equity closed-end funds from this sponsor have a significant amount of technology exposure.

The fund’s website states that the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund only has 20.71% of its portfolio invested in technology:

However, this is somewhat misleading because some of the companies that many people consider to be technology are not classified in this manner under the Global Industry Classification Standard. Alphabet, for example, is classified as Communication Services, not Information Technology. Here are the GICS classifications for each of the companies in ETO’s top ten holdings:

Company Name

GICS Sector

Alphabet

Communications Services

NVIDIA

Information Technology

Micron Technology

Information Technology

Microsoft

Information Technology

Amazon.com

Consumer Discretionary

ASML Holding

Information Technology

Apple

Information Technology

AstraZeneca (AZN)

Healthcare

Societe Generale (SCGLF)

Financials

Banco Santander (SAN)

Financials

If we total the weightings for only the companies classified as Information Technology under the GICS system that are included among the fund’s largest positions, we get 13.37%. This is lower than the 20.71% weighting that the fund’s website states that this fund has to that sector. As such, there must be more technology companies included in the fund’s portfolio than just the ones that we see above. The fund’s first-quarter holdings report confirms this, as it lists Broadcom (AVGO), Infineon Technologies (IFNNY), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), among others. Alternatively, if we consider Alphabet and Amazon to be technology companies, as many investors do, then this fund’s exposure to technology is greater than the 20.71% weighting that the website provides.

One of the characteristics of technology companies is that they tend to have relatively low yields. This is because, at least historically, these companies tended to retain most or all of their free cash flow to use to finance growth. That still appears to be true today, which we can see by looking at the yields of the largest companies in this fund’s portfolio:

Company Name

Current Yield

Alphabet

0.28%

NVIDIA

0.02%

Micron Technology

0.16%

Microsoft

0.98%

Amazon.com

n/a

ASML Holding

0.67%

Apple

0.40%

AstraZeneca (AZN)

1.58%

Societe Generale (SCGLF)

2.69%

Banco Santander (SAN)

2.43%

The only common stocks out of the top ten holdings in the fund’s portfolio that have yields higher than 1% are in the healthcare and financial sectors. None of these common equities have yields that are above the 3.2% to 3.5% or so yields that can be obtained from a money market fund. Indeed, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is using the Morgan Stanley Institutional Liquidity Funds – Government Portfolio Institutional Class (MVRXX) to store its short-term cash holdings:

The schedule of investments states that this fund has a 3.61% yield, but the fact sheet for that fund states that it has a 30-day current yield of 3.57%. In either case, this money market fund has a higher yield than any of the common equities that comprise ETO’s ten largest positions. If we were to assume that the yields of the fund’s ten largest positions are comparable to the rest of its common equity portfolio, then this fund could earn a higher yield by selling off the common stocks and just putting the money into the money market fund.

The fund’s annual report seems to confirm that the fund’s portfolio as a whole has a very low yield. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund reported dividend income of $15,184,092 (not including the distributions that it received from the money market fund) on an average net asset value of $481,510,736. That only works out to a 3.15% yield. Admittedly, though, the fund’s average net asset value would have included common stocks, preferred stocks, and bonds. Thus, the average yield that the fund received from the common stocks alone would be a bit higher than this calculated figure. We can still see, however, that its income from the common stocks is not very impressive.

Of course, the bulk of common stock returns comes from price appreciation rather than dividends. This is immediately apparent if we compare the S&P 500 Index to the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) over the past ten years:

The S&P 500 Index only shows the price level of the index. However, the S&P 500 Total Return Index makes the assumption that a hypothetical investor in the index reinvests all of the dividends that were paid out by all of the constituent companies. We can see that adding in the dividends only increased the cumulative return over the ten-year period by 58.33%. Thus, the majority of the index’s returns came solely from price appreciation.

The fact that common stocks deliver price appreciation over time is also one way through which they are able to provide their investors with protection against inflation. As we all know, inflation is a broad-based increase in the price of everything that we buy as either businesses or consumers. If we hold assets that also rise in price at least as quickly as consumer goods rise, then presumably we could sell them at any time and be able to purchase the same items that we could have purchased when we bought the asset. Theoretically, the prices of consumer goods could rise an unlimited amount, and that has actually been seen during hyperinflation episodes that have occurred throughout history. Common stock prices can also theoretically rise an unlimited amount. After all, inflation will generally push an issuing company’s revenues and profits up when measured in nominal currency. Fixed-income securities, on the other hand, do not have unlimited upside potential due to the simple fact that there is a point at which the yield provided by the security becomes negative in real terms, and few investors are actually willing to purchase a security with a negative real yield. Overall, then, common equities can do a better job at protecting an investor’s wealth against the loss of purchasing power that accompanies inflation than any type of fixed-income security can.

This could be important because there are signs that inflation is likely to get worse in the near future. We can see this by looking at the Producer Price Index. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics states:

The Producer Price Index program measures the average change over time in the selling prices received by domestic producers for their output. The prices included in the PPI are from the first commercial transaction for many products and some services.

This index basically tells us the actual prices that were paid by retail stores as well as by intermediary companies (for example, a car manufacturer purchasing parts from its suppliers before assembling them). As such, this index can give us a good idea of where consumer prices might be heading as the businesses will eventually have to pass price increases through to their customers. This chart shows the month-over-month change in the Producer Price Index for every month in the past year:

The major trend that we notice here is that the rate of change has been accelerating since October of 2025, with each month coming in worse than the previous month. The most recent figure was a 0.7% month-over-month increase, which works out to 8.4% annualized. It is highly unlikely that any business will continue to absorb price increases of this magnitude without passing them through to customers. After all, with the exception of businesses with very high margins, they might not even be able to absorb them and still remain profitable.

Another index that can be very important to watch to get an idea of where inflation might be headed is the US Import/Export Price Indices. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics states:

The International Price Program produces Import/Export Price Indexes containing data on changes in the prices of nonmilitary goods and services traded between the U.S. and the rest of the world.

In other words, these indices measure the prices that are actually being paid by entities that import goods into the United States or received by entities that export goods from the United States. In February, import prices increased by 1.3% month-over-month following a 0.6% month-over-month increase in January. For exports, the figures were 1.5% month-over-month in February following a 0.6% increase in January:

If we annualize the February numbers, we get price increases of 15.6% for imports and 20.4% for exports. As with the Producer Price Index numbers, it is highly unlikely that businesses will simply absorb price increases of this level without passing them on to consumers. We should also note that both the PPI and the Import/Export numbers are from February, before the recent surge in crude oil prices. Now that the Iran War has made fuel more expensive, we can expect further upward pressure on prices.

The takeaway here is that there is a very real possibility that consumer inflation will surge in the near future. Personally, I would not be surprised if it starts to show up in the CPI over the summer. This suggests that investors would be wise to begin acquiring assets that offer protection against inflation. While common equities would be a much better choice for this than fixed-income securities, the fact that the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has high exposure to both technology and financials could be problematic. As we saw in 2022, common stocks issued by technology companies do not always do well in inflationary environments. This is particularly true if the Federal Reserve reacts to the inflationary surge with an increase in interest rates. I doubt that the central bank will do any more than a quarter-point or half-point increase at the most because the high U.S. federal debt and fiscal deficits require interest rates to remain low, so the most that I would expect is a half-hearted interest rate increase so that the Federal Reserve can claim that it is trying to fight inflation. Technology company common stocks might still be adversely affected, though, because they are long-duration securities and some of the largest technology companies are actually borrowing large amounts of money to finance the construction of data centers for artificial intelligence. Rising interest rates will make this borrowing more expensive. Financials, on the other hand, actually benefit from rising interest rates, but it is still very possible that they would get sold off too if investors simply start selling equity index funds in response to rising interest rates.

As such, the fund’s portfolio might not be the best if companies start to pass rising costs through to consumers and the Federal Reserve attempts to raise interest rates to contain inflation. With that said, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has 36.61% of its portfolio invested in foreign common equities, which could hold up better than American ones in such an event. In addition, I would much rather be holding a portfolio that is overweight common equities than one that has a lot of bond exposure. Investors who own ETO might want to prepare themselves for some volatility, however.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund was on June 17, 2025. That was almost ten months ago, and shares of the fund are up 4.05% since that time:

As we can see, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund underperformed both the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI All-Countries World Index (ACWI) over the period, but given that this is a hybrid fund that includes both common stocks and bonds, this is not unexpected. After all, bonds typically underperform stocks, so we would expect the presence of the bonds in the portfolio to weaken the fund’s performance relative to an all-stock index like the S&P 500 or the MSCI ACWI.

On a total return performance, ETO also underperformed both indices:

As we can see quite clearly, investors in the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund have experienced a 9.95% total return since the last time that we discussed this fund. That is still a bit worse than both equity indices, but a 9.95% total return over ten months is not really too bad.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of total return, which makes sense for a fund that has most of its portfolio invested in common equities. However, most closed-end funds deliver the bulk of their total returns by paying regular distributions out to their investors. The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is no exception to this, as the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.1733 per share ($2.0796 per share annually). This gives the fund a 7.63% yield at the current share price.

The fund released its annual report a few months ago, which corresponds to the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025. While the closing date of this report is still a few months in the past, it is a newer report than what we had available to us the last time that we looked at this fund. Therefore, let us have a look at it today and see how well this fund is covering its distributions.

For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund reported a net investment income of $10,612,821. That was not sufficient to cover the $34,080,772 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period. Admittedly, on the surface, this may be concerning, but in actuality, very few funds that have the majority of their assets invested in common equities are able to fully cover their distributions through net investment income. This is mostly because of the very low yields that common equities typically possess.

Fortunately, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, ETO reported net realized gains of $22,498,495 along with $55,154,431 worth of net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund’s portfolio provided total nominal returns of $88,265,747 over the period:

This was obviously enough to cover distributions totaling $34,080,772. However, we can see that the fund had to rely slightly on its unrealized gains to achieve full coverage. The total of the fund’s net investment income and net realized gains is only $33,111,316. That was not enough to cover the distributions. As such, this fund had to use $969,456 of its unrealized gains to cover the distribution. Ordinarily, it can be problematic for any closed-end fund to use unrealized gains to offset the distributions due to the simple fact that unrealized gains can be erased by the market. However, in this case, it is probably okay because the fund got so close to covering the distributions solely with its realized income and gains that it is unlikely to have its unrealized gains erased so sufficiently as to have the distributions prove destructive to its net asset value.

Overall, there does not appear to be any reason to worry about the sustainability of the fund’s distribution. It appears to be reasonably safe.

Valuation

Shares of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund are currently trading at a 10.76% discount on net asset value. This is a slightly worse price than the 11.28% discount that the shares of this fund have had on average over the past month.

The fund’s current price is, however, very reasonable compared to its long-term historical average valuation. Over the past three years, shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from an 11.63% discount to a 15.57% premium on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 5.60% discount on net asset value. The fund’s current price is, therefore, very reasonable compared to its own historical valuation.

Here is how the current price of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Opportunities Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Expenses

The fund’s website states that the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has an expense ratio of 2.26%:
