data.title: Option Activity In Microsoft Points To Higher Prices (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Note: We use option activity to determine “when” to buy or sell—not “what” to buy or sell. The “what” decision is best made through fundamental analysis. Once an investor has decided "what" to buy or sell, I would never act without first reviewing what option activity was saying about its immediate price direction.

Microsoft's Headed Higher For The Next Few Months

Current option activity in Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) strongly suggests higher prices over the intermediate-term. The intermediate term is from two to five months.

If you're bullish on Microsoft for the long-term, now is a good time to add to positions. If you're bearish, you'll probably get a higher selling price if you wait a few months. Let me show you the evidence. While this data is one week old (subscribers get the current data on 100 stocks), the article's conclusion for a strong intermediate-term buy signal in MSFT still stands.

The “Puts To Calls” Ratio

Marty Zweig introduced the put-to-call ratio more than fifty years ago. He compared purchases of “put” and “call” option contracts to gauge whether investors were bullish or bearish on the market. He observed that when “too many” investors bought calls, prices typically declined. When “too many” bought puts, prices generally increased. This activity functioned as a contrary sentiment indicator.

While Zweig focused on contracts, we analyze capital flows, which we believe provides greater insight. We call our ratio the Directional Premium Puts to Calls Ratio.

Directional “Puts To Calls” Ratio Of MSFT

The graph below measures the puts-to-calls ratio of dollars flowing into directional puts versus directional calls in MSFT. We average the numbers over 20 days to smooth the ratio into a more meaningful intermediate-term indicator. The Red Zone - Green Zone ranking shows extremes by comparing ratios against their historic norms.

It's a buy signal when you get extreme bearish expectations (Green Zone) and a sell signal when you get extreme bullish expectations (Red Zone).

Green Zone readings mean an excessive amount of money is buying puts, while Red Zone means an excessive amount of money is buying calls. A Green Zone signal of minus 9 or less is an intermediate-term buy signal, while Red Zone signals of +9 or more are sell signals. Like with all contrary opinion indicators, buy signals are more reliable than sell signals.

As you can see, the current amount of directional put buying compared to calls in Microsoft equals that of the bear market lows of 2022. It exceeds the levels of last April and the 2020 COVID bear market. This is bullish.

Ratio Of Directional Puts And Call Trades

To make sure the reading of the premium ratio accurately reflects what traders think and is not due to one or two large trades, we form a similar ratio of the number of put and call trades. The indicators should look similar. That's the graph below.

You can see that the ratio of put and call trades is similar to the premium ratio, which means the extreme premium ratio is not due to one or two large trades.

Total Directional Put Buying

The chart below shows the total amount of money going into directional puts on an average day. While the puts-to-calls ratio compares how much money's going into puts versus calls, this chart tells you the amount of money going into directional puts. Ideally, you want both metrics—the ratio and the absolute amount—to be near extremes. And you do.

There is currently over $300 million a day going into directional puts, expecting Microsoft stock to decline. This level of put buying is unprecedented, being five times more than the bear market lows of 2022.

The Microsoft VIX

Stocks have a VIX index just like the market. The normal VIX is the 30-day implied volatility of the S&P 500. Peaks in implied volatility usually occur at price lows. The chart below is a graph of the Microsoft VIX. It's graphed from 2018 to the present. We use a 90-day implied volatility since it’s more regular.

You can see the 90-day implied volatility of Microsoft exceeds the number reached last April, and it's approaching levels that occurred during the bear market lows of 2020.

It could go higher, but these levels are bullish.

Microsoft SKEW

This chart below shows the 20-day average of Microsoft’s SKEW. The SKEW measures the difference in implied volatility traders are giving 30-day, out-of-the-money puts versus calls of Microsoft.

Implied volatility for puts and calls should theoretically be the same, but in practice it isn’t—and that difference carries information. The CBOE measures this difference, normalizes it, centers it at zero, and then tracks deviations from that baseline. This measure is known as SKEW. We take the daily SKEW and average it over 20 days to create an intermediate-term view.

A high reading above zero indicates traders are overpaying for out-of-the-money puts, reflecting excessive bearishness, which typically appears near market bottoms. We are getting a peak in the SKEW with the recent decline, but as you can see, it’s not yet at the level reached last April or in 2022.

Summary

The amount of money going into directional puts versus calls equals the bear market lows of 2022 and the COVID bear market of 2020. This is confirmed by the companion ratio of the number of put trades versus call trades.

With the Iran sell-off, the actual amount of money going into directional puts is five times higher than it was during the bear market low of 2022.

The implied volatility of the price of Microsoft options, called the Microsoft VIX, is almost at the levels reached at the lows of 2022 and 2020.

The Microsoft SKEW is approaching levels reached at past lows but, in our opinion, has further to go.

This evidence strongly supports an intermediate-term rally in MSFT of from two to five months.

Investors who are bullish on Microsoft for the long-term should take this opportunity to add to positions. Investors who are bearish long-term should wait a few months to liquidate at higher prices.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: The Rally Makes Sense But So Does More Upside
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) stock has surged 17.3% since my last report and now trades ahead of my base case price target of $31.41 and just slightly below my bullish $33.34 price target. While the company’s business model largely shields it from the commodity price fluctuations, the situation in the Middle East may have some positive consequences, which I discuss in this report along with an update on the price target.

The Opportunities And Risks For Kinder Morgan

Oil prices have increased 75% since the start of the war, while natural gas prices in the EU have spiked nearly 60%. The company’s exposure is 65% to natural gas, 9% to carbon dioxide and 26% to refined products. Kinder Morgan is largely shielded against volatility in commodity prices, but there still are opportunities.

Kinder Morgan is only partially exposed to fluctuations in commodity prices. So, on 5% of their price-based business they can benefit from higher commodity prices. 64% of their business is take-or-pay, which is basically a capacity lease. Regardless of whether a customer uses reserved capacity it pays a fee. 26% of the business is volume-based, and that may be where the bigger benefit lies for Kinder Morgan. With supply from the Middle East choked, countries may look elsewhere for their natural gas imports, and as the company that moves 40% of the U.S. natural gas production through 55,000 miles of natural gas pipelines, Kinder Morgan is positioned well to benefit from a potential uplift in production. Furthermore, the company derives cash flow from refined product streaming. So, what could benefit Kinder Morgan is not so much the change in the commodity prices but the secondary effect where demand for U.S. gas and refined products increases, which leads to higher volumes boosting the volume-based business and may also strengthen pricing power on new take-or-pay contracts.

There are also risks, which include that oil and gas prices are so high that economic growth may slow, which essentially would hurt the company’s volume-based business.

At the same time, we note that names with predictable cash flows and stability in shareholder returns may attract investors as the stock market is generally more volatile. That also explains the surge in the stock price, but did result in the dividend yield compressing from 4.2% to 3.5%.

Kinder Morgan Still Has Upside

If we look at the revision on the estimates for revenues and earnings per share, we note that there hasn’t been any meaningful change in estimates. For the first quarter, revenues were revised up by 0.9% and by 5.4% on earnings per share. In the remaining quarters this year, estimates have come down by 0.9%-1.5% and earnings per share have not changed. For the full year summing each quarter would show a 0.7% reduction in revenue estimates and a 1.5% increase in EPS. Those numbers do not add up to the numbers analysts provided for their annual estimates, but we see a 0.1% downward revision on revenues for 2026 and a 1.5% increase in EPS while for 2027 the revenue estimates decreased by 0.6% and up by 0.9% for 2028 with no change in EPS estimates.

That makes it extremely important to assess whether the valuation for Kinder Morgan remains attractive. To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For midstream companies, I am looking for around 7% upside with forward earnings in mind, and Kinder Morgan still satisfies that requirement. Even with high oil and gas prices, we don’t see estimates showing any substantial growth in the years ahead. Sales are expected to grow 3.9% annually with 4% EBITDA growth and free cash flow growth compressing due to higher capital expenditures. I am maintaining my buy rating for Kinder Morgan with a $35.92 price target, noting that this year’s earnings are not fully factored in.

For Kinder Morgan, EBITDA margins are expected to dip by 2027 and then curl up again with cash conversion pressures on higher capital expenditures against operating cash flow. The yield on cost is expected to rise to 3.8%, up from the current yield of 3.5%.

Overall, I do feel comfortable with my buy rating even with a less than 10% upside. You could make the case for Kinder Morgan to start trading ahead of its median valuation. If we were to apply a 10% premium to the EV/EBITDA, we would get price targets of $38.90-$41.01 indicating 17%-24% upside. That would be an extremely bullish valuation scenario, but it is good to keep in mind what a possible expansion to the trading multiple would do to the upside.

Conclusion: Kinder Morgan Still Stock Looks Attractive

While Kinder Morgan’s share prices have significantly appreciated, we note that this merely closed the valuation gap towards 2025-2026 and does not include any upside from earnings in 2027 or any positive reset on the trading multiple. For now, I am maintaining my buy rating as I believe that the company has incremental upside with growing dividend prospects.

data.title: Alphabet: Q1 Is The Moment Of Truth
data.description:
Every quarter, Wall Street breaks down Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL/GOOG) by segments, which is why the company draws so much attention. There’s always something to discuss and argue about. And now, with the first-quarter 2026 earnings potentially on Apr. 29, 2026, the stock will be coming back into the limelight after a brutal selloff that doesn’t align with the foundation of the underlying business.

As a shareholder, I have many reasons to be optimistic, which I’m excited to cover today. With that, and everything else outlined below, my Strong Buy rating for Alphabet stays in place.

Where does Alphabet stock stand?

Before anything else, let’s set the stage with where the stock sits today to help frame its trajectory.

At the time of this publication, Alphabet last traded at around $305 per share, which puts it roughly 13% below its all-time high of about $350 per share set in early February this year, while the stock is modestly in the red zone on a year-to-date basis. That said, zooming out shows the share price has more than doubled over the past 12 months.

It’s also worth noting that Alphabet was among the few companies that were less affected by the widespread growth stock selloff that’s been going on since late 2025, which potentially shows that the market woke up to the idea that Alphabet’s AI push isn’t a lab experiment, but something that can actually pay off over time.

Going back, what’s behind the near-term weakness? Well, we had a perfect storm, with the center being the tension between the U.S. and Iran, which sent oil prices flying and triggered a broad selloff in growth stocks. That said, Alphabet was less affected.

What did we learn in Alphabet’s Q4 ’25 earnings call?

To see where a business is going, we first have to understand where it’s been. In Alphabet’s case, Q4’s numbers were as solid as ever.

Revenue came in at $113.8 billion, rising 18% from the same quarter last year. Full year revenue crossed $400 billion, a new record, was up 15%. On the bottom line, net income for the quarter rose 30% to ~$34.5 billion. For the full year, it came in at $132 billion. More importantly, both top and bottom line figures beat the consensus estimates by a wide margin.

If we break down the segments, we can see that Google Services revenue came in at $95.9 billion, up 14%. Revenue from Google Search was up 17% to and YouTube revenue rose 9%. What I can’t understand is how so many are sleeping on Alphabet’s Search numbers. It wasn’t too long ago that pretty much everyone was saying Google Ads were going the way of the Do-Do now that so many of us are using LLM’s to get our answers.

But as I’ve said before, it’s hard to change habits. When I search, I start by going to Google, not OpenAI's (OPENAI) ChatGPT. And if anything, data shows that Google’s AI Overviews and AI Mode are keeping users on Google Search longer, which ultimately opens up new ways for the company to serve ads.

Of course, the showstopper in the quarter was Google Cloud. Revenue in the segment jumped 48% to $17.7 billion- up 30%. And cloud operating income more than doubled to ~$5.3 billion. Operating margins also expanded from 17.5% to “just” over 30%.

Zooming out, operating income grew 16%, and operating margin was 31.6%. Although impressive on its own, both numbers were dragged down by a $2.1 billion compensation charge tied to Waymo. This is largely because Waymo’s valuation jumped after a $16 billion investment round, the significant majority of which was funded by Alphabet, valuing the self-driving business at $126 billion, and when a subsidiary’s value jumps like that, the stock-based compensation owed to employees gets marked up on the books.

That same dynamic resulted in Alphabet’s Other Bets segment’s reported revenue declining 8% to $370 million compared to the same period last year, and operating loss ballooned to $3.6 billion from $1.17 billion. Nearly all of that traces back to the same Waymo charge, so if you strip it out, the operating picture looks a lot cleaner than the headlines suggest.

On the spending side, fourth-quarter capex in at $27.9 billion, while full-year spending reached a total of $91.4 billion in 2025, which was split roughly 60/40 between servers and data center infrastructure. On the balance sheet, Alphabet closed the year with $126.8 billion in cash and generated $52.4 billion in operating cash flow in the fourth quarter alone.

Of course, the number that spooked a lot of us: Alphabet’s 2026 capex guidance between $175 billion and $185 billion, or roughly double the 2025 figures. All of it is earmarked for AI compute capacity across DeepMind, Google Services, Cloud, and Other Bets, while Wall Street expected 2026 spending to be closer to $120 billion. That surprise is what spooked the market, which is also the biggest reason the stock sold off on earnings day.

That’s practically what happened to Alphabet in the fourth quarter, so with that in place, let’s look into the company’s most important potential growth driver over the next 12 to 18 months.

What could drive Alphabet higher from here?

As you might’ve expected, Google Cloud remains the growth driver heading into the first quarter of 2026.

A 48% revenue growth rate from a segment already running at an annualized pace of over $70 billion is impressive, but more importantly, what makes this segment a growth driver is the demand pipeline that supports it. Cloud backlog increased more than twofold year over year to $240 billion compared to the same period last year. This is the contracted revenue that’s already been signed but has yet to show up in the financials.

The backlog alone implies roughly 3 to 4 years of current revenue just waiting to be recognized. Also, the number of billion-dollar-plus deals in 2025 surpassed the prior 3 years combined, and existing customers are spending over 30% more than what they originally committed to. That’s because Google Cloud’s AI infrastructure customers are finding more ways to use the platform faster than expected. That might be the clearest signal of product-market fit one can ask for at this point.

On the cost side, the economics appear to be getting better, and fast. As mentioned during Alphabet’s fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call, Gemini’s serving costs fell 78% over 2025. In practice, this means that as backlog materializes into revenue, each new dollar should be able to squeeze out more margin than in the fourth quarter. What’s not to love about that?

If Cloud can push past 30% operating margins while sustaining 40%+ growth, this segment alone could be generating north of $25 billion in annual operating income within 18 to 24 months, and that would meaningfully change Alphabet’s picture.

Indeed, there are plenty of deals to support this runway. For example, back in January, Apple (AAPL) and Alphabet announced a partnership where the next generation of Apple’s AI models will be built on Google’s Gemini technology and cloud infrastructure. According to the report, Apple is paying roughly $1 billion a year to license a custom Gemini model, with a goal of fundamentally upgrading Siri- as you might’ve noticed, Siri has been lagging behind its peers despite having a better head start.

Reports also suggest that Apple planned to let Alphabet set up servers inside Apple’s own data centers to handle Siri’s processing. That should tell you a lot about the level of trust between these two giants, but more importantly, this partnership puts Alphabet’s technologies at the center of one of the world’s largest consumer device ecosystems.

Also, Alphabet’s deal with Broadcom (AVGO) expanded significantly just yesterday. That’s because Broadcom announced a new 5-year agreement to develop future versions of Alphabet’s Tensor Processing Units, or TPUs. Basically, they’re custom-designed AI chips, for those who are unfamiliar with them.

And within this sphere, Anthropic signed an expanded compute deal with Alphabet and Broadcom for multiple gigawatts of TPU capacity. The first gigawatt arrives by the end of 2026, and scales to 3.5 gigawatts by 2027. You see, there’s a lot more to Alphabet’s partnerships to tackle, and I might’ve digressed a little, but the point is that all these partnerships support the materialization of Alphabet’s existing backlog of over $240 billion.

So, what should we look for in Alphabet’s first-quarter 2026 earnings? Well, it comes down to two things. Whether Google Cloud can keep growing at 48%, or better yet, push even higher, and whether the backlog keeps stacking up. That’s because if those two factors hold while margins stay above 30%, it’ll be a good sign that management’s capex plan isn’t just a leap of faith. It might actually pay dividends sooner than expected.

But…what could go wrong?

Well, the growth trajectory is strong, but there are a few thorny issues heading into the first quarter that could put some pressure on the stock. And a big one is the depreciation wave, as management confirmed during the fourth-quarter call. According to CFO Anat Ashkenazi:

Given the increase in our CapEx investments in recent years, we expect the growth rate in 2026 depreciation to accelerate in Q1 and meaningfully increase for the full year. We're also planning to continue hiring in key investment areas such as AI and cloud.

When Alphabet acquires all that data-center-building equipment, the cost doesn’t hit the income statement all at once. Instead, it gets expensed over the asset’s useful life as a line item called depreciation. The cash goes out today, but the expense trickles through over several years.

And, this is why it’s important. Alphabet’s 2025 depreciation charges rose 38% to $21.1 billion compared to the previous year. Basically, this is the accounting charge that spreads the cost of significant purchases like servers and buildings over their useful life, and that $21.1 billion came off $91.4 billion of total capex for that year.

Now, for 2026, Alphabet is guiding between $175 and $185 billion in spending, or nearly double that of 2025. If the ratio holds, depreciation could over $30 billion for the entire year, which adds an extra ~$10 billion in non-cash costs to the income statement. Ashkenazi also pointed out that operating costs for the data center segment, especially energy costs, will be added on top, which means we can expect more pressure should the war in Iran continue. Today, though, it seems like we might have a little reprieve thanks to the ceasefire.

Circling back, the first-quarter 2026 report is where the depreciation wave starts showing up - for real. Back in Q4, operating income increased 16% on 18% revenue growth, with margins at 31.6%. You see, profit growth is already trailing revenue growth by 2 percentage points, and that was before the 2026 spending ramps up.

So, what matters most in Q1 is whether that gap gets wider. Because if it does, it means the spending is outpacing revenue, which is a disconnect that would potentially weigh on the stock even when the top line looks healthy.

That said, the depreciation wave is the unavoidable price tag for building data centers that cater to demand, so the question boils down to whether the market has the patience to wait several quarters for the payoff to show up in the reported numbers. I’ve owned GOOGL for almost 16 years, and I’ll probably continue to for decades to come. So it’s easy to say that none of this is an issue for those with horizons of more than three years, but impatient investors may have a different idea.

What’s my verdict for Alphabet?

Zooming out, Alphabet looks like a company that has quietly become one of the most important and diversified names in tech. And while everyone’s talking about which AI model will dominate, Alphabet is selling the cloud infrastructure and the enterprise platform that all those models need to actually run in production.

It doesn’t matter whether the winning AI model right now is built by a startup on some small island, in the U.S., or in a London vault. If it’s running at scale, there’s a good chance that it’s running on Google Cloud. And while the market is still focused on whether Alphabet’s capex is too high, it's quickly becoming an essential service for startups and enterprises.

For that reason, and all else outlined above, my Strong Buy rating for Alphabet remains unchanged.

data.title: Snap: Underwriting Tail Risk For A 21% Yield Ahead Of An Inevitable Restructuring
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Snap recently had a substantial setback with its stock price due to massive stock-based compensation (SBC) and Specs cash burn, which has sent the stock down well below its historical average. The market's disappointment has not only driven down its stock price, but also pushed implied volatility (IV) of options to high levels. However, investors ignore the fact that Snap is a special asset. It can be a highly profitable company if it changes. Furthermore, if the stock price falls excessively to current levels, change is inevitable. The management has acknowledged this and taken action, taking a step to establish Specs as a distinct subsidiary within Snap Inc. in Feb 2026. This situation has attracted me to try to create an options strategy with a very thick safety margin and an annualized return of up to 21%. In the following analysis, I will delve into the underlying logic behind this trade.

Business Overview

Snap includes two business units: Snapchat and Specs.

SNAPCHAT

Snapchat is a very good and popular product, as highlighted by Irenic Capital in their “Snap Back to Reality” presentation. Advantages of Snapchat include:

  • 1 of only 4 scaled U.S. social networks

  • ~1bn MAUs and 474mm DAUs –Huge, irreplaceable scale

  • $6bn in revenue growing 10%+ YoY

  • $1bn in subscription revenues growing 60%+ YoY

  • Unique ability to captivate user attention and media trends

  • Proprietary data assets and advertising stack –one of the few “walled gardens”

The current price of Snap is $4.63, implying that TEV is only $8.4 Bn, and TEV‑to‑Sales ratio is 1.4x. Compared to its competitors, it is extremely undervalued (Reddit is 6.6x, Meta is 5.2x, Pinterest is 2.0x), which is the consensus in the market. These undeniable facts form the basis for all the following discussion.

Specs

Specs is another story. On the one hand, it is the technological dream of Evan Spiegel. In the letter of “Crucible Moment”, Evan Spiegel describes it as "human-centered computing" , claiming that:

"Specs represent a shift away from the app paradigm to an AI-first experience... into a once-in-a-generation transformation towards human-centered computing."

However, on the other hand, financially, it is a high-risk project with no near-term profitability. According to Irenic's estimates, a total of $3.5 billion was invested in R&D over 11 years, and by 2025, an estimated $500 million in cash plus $200 million in SBC had been invested. Furthermore, CEO Evan Spiegel himself has publicly admitted that a true commercial breakthrough for AR glasses won't happen until the end of the decade (2030).

Reasons for the stock price setback

Snapchat and Specs are two completely different businesses. The company used operational cash from SNAPCHAT and a large amount of SBC to support Specs. This kept the company in a negative profit state for a long time, and its stock price fell by 77% compared to its IPO. Shareholder returns gave way to the founders' technological dreams.

At the same time, the founders held 20% of the shares but had 99% of the voting rights. This voting structure meant that external shareholders could not effectively impact the founders when the company neglected shareholder returns. This also lowered the company's valuation.

Catalyst for change

In SNAP's business model, SBC is a key factor. We can see from the table as following:

Annual stock-based compensation expense is approximately $1 billion. If paid in cash alternatively, EBITDA in 2025 would be -$320 million, and operating cash flow would become - $340 million, which will close all debt financing channels for the company.

According to estimates, Specs' cash burn in 2025 will be approximately $700 million, meaning that most of the cash generated by SBC will be used for Specs. If the SBC plan is unsustainable, Specs will also lose a stable source of funding.

However, the sustainability of the SBC plan depends on one key condition: a reasonably high stock price, allowing earnings growth to significantly outpace share dilution.

If we look at the SBC dilution rate in different stock price's condition, it is easy to demonstrate why the status quo is mathematically unsustainable.

Assuming: annual SBC Expense: $1.0 billion (Fixed), Total Shares Outstanding: 1.69 billion

Snap’s SBC Dilution & Required Growth vs Stock Price

As shown in the table above, if the stock price remains at the current price of $4.60, the dilution rate reaches 12.86%, which is four times the dilution rate of 3% in 2025. This means that theoretically, EBITDA growth needs to be significantly higher than 12.86% for the stock price to remain unchanged. In an extreme case, if the stock price falls to $3, the dilution rate reaches 20%, meaning that theoretically, EBITDA growth needs to be significantly higher than 20%.

If the EBITDA growth cannot be met with a high dilution rate , the stock price will continue to decline. On the one hand, the SBC plan will become unsustainable, and Specs will lose a stable source of funding. On the other hand, the value of employee stock options will continuously shrink, severely damaging morale and causing talent loss. In that case, Specs will also fail.

Our opinion is that in this situation, Evan Spiegel will inevitably make structural changes to balance company profitability, shareholder returns, and technological dream; otherwise, it will be a lose-lose situation.

Evan Spiegel is a Silicon Valley genius, but he could get carried away with his technological dreams. However, he also has extensive business experience, and Snap has an experienced board of directors. He completely understands it, emphasizing this point in as follows:

Achieving net income profitability would also help offset the dilution risk of stock-based compensation and establish a stronger foundation under our share price. These are the facts of our crucible moment.

From letter to employees “crucible moment” in 2025

We continued to manage our share count carefully, with share repurchases completed throughout 2025 helping limit share count growth to 3% in Q4.

From Snap Investor letter Q4 2025

Snap has spent $500 million repurchasing shares to support stock price and offset the dilution. Also declare a new $500 million repurchasing plan in 2026. All of this proves that the company recognizes the impact of stock price on SNAP's business model.

The Path Forward

Snapchat is a top-tier social media product with 1 billion MAUs. If management focuses on software monetization and cost control, the company should be able to easily achieve profitability. Furthermore, Snapchat has strong growth potential in subscription revenues and data licensing. If the cash-burning Specs business were spun out, as an independently operating company, Snapchat would become a highly profitable company with high free cash flow, thereby unlocking the company's value.

Meanwhile, based on current predictions, Specs is unlikely to achieve cash flow breakeven before 2030. A rational approach would be to spin it out and operate it as a startup. Operating independently, Specs could rationally assess its business prospects and optimize efficiency based on hardware product development cycles; at the same time, it can obtain a suitable valuation. Attracting external capital and establishing its own SBC system.

The company has recognized this and taken action, taking an important step to establish Specs Inc. as a distinct subsidiary within Snap Inc. in Feb 2026.

Specs faces a challenging environment in the current capital markets, particularly regarding securing independent funding and establishing its own SBC system. However, this actually approves our opinion: if external capital declines to fund Specs, it will put its future commercial viability to a real test. Without external funding, together with the unsustainable nature of Snap's internal SBC system due to the depressed stock price, management will be forced to significantly reduce or even halt Specs investment. Therefore, regardless of whether Specs ultimately thrives as an independent company or is forced to scale down due to funding shortages, the final result is the same: cash burn stops. Which will unlock the value of Snap's core business. This effectively limited the further downside potential of Snap's stock price.

My Strategy: Execution Plan to Capture Risk Premium Using Asset Dislocation

I sold Cash-Secured Puts with a $3.00 strike price. The expiration date is Jan 2027. The option premium is $0.36. If unexercised, the total annualized return is >21%. If the exercise occurs, the actual entry price is $2.64 per share. In that scenario, I am acquiring Snap at a $5.6 billion TEV.

In my strategy, I always act like a stock price risk insurer. If the exercise occurs, my logic is the same as that of a non-performing asset disposal company.

Execution Details and Return Analysis

I sold Cash-Secured Puts with a $3.00 strike price. The expiration date is Jan 2027. Under the premise of 100% cash collateral (zero leverage), the annualized return of this option is 17%. Total Return: If adding the current 4% margin short-term T-bill interest income, the total annualized yield is >21%. This is a highly asymmetric risk underwriting opportunity based on extreme low TEV plus high IV in the current market environment.

Internal Logic: Abnormal Premium of Implied Volatility (IV)

The current implied volatility (IV) of Snap reaches 80%. It means that the market thinks the possibility of a "cash burn out plus more share dilution" is very high in Snap. The 1.4 TEV‑to‑Sales ratio has priced the stock with high risk. At the same time, high IV priced the options wrongly again. According to my analysis, the $4 Snap price is far below the bottom line required for both Snap’s SBC operation modes and Specs’s survival. The probability of this happening is extremely small. The high IV is the market paying too high a premium for "extreme tail risk of burning money endlessly without structural change." It is obviously an overpricing of risk.

Risk Bottom Line Test Breakeven Point

The breakeven point for this strategy is $2.64. At that time, the company's TEV was only $5.6 billion. TEV‑to‑Sales ratio is 0.93

Assignment Preparation

In extreme cases, even if the option is exercised, I am acquiring Snap with $5.6 billion in TEV. I believe I still can get good returns in the end. At this time, I am in non-performing asset disposal company mode.

I have built real $3.00 strike Put positions. This position is part of my "Special Situations Arbitrage" portfolio. This portfolio includes 30 uncorrelated targets with similar logic. I hold this position without regard to short-term stock price fluctuations. My target is to generate 20%+ annual underwriting profit by identifying asset pricing, risk pricing error and Potential catalysts. In the extreme case of Snap, I think an annual return of 21%+ can far cover the actual loss for potential risks. This is one of my typical ways to get high annualized returns with actual low risk in special situations arbitrage.

data.title: Target: My Target Price Justifies The Recent Rally, Pause For Now
data.description:
If you remember, I advised you not to do anything after Target Corporation (TGT) rose and delivered 12% returns in my previous coverage. I admit that I spoke both as an analyst and as an investor in this company as I suggested to existing investors not to sell their shares and to newcomers not to touch TGT yet. My view of its valuation paid off as the price reached my target price using the Dividend Discount Model. But of course, I realized I was a bit too early to downgrade it. And now that the market is becoming more volatile amid riskier external factors, I believe that it's essential to revisit this stock. Valuation and technicals now appear more synchronized as upside potential now appears limited.

Target: Some Strengths Amid Challenges

Macroeconomic volatility has remained intense in recent months. Although inflation has slowed down, it remains above the 2.0% Fed target. In addition, stubborn inflation means prices are still increasing. So, this also continues to squeeze purchasing power as well as pricing flexibility of businesses. Retailers like Target Corporation are not safe from these headwinds. If you look back to my previous coverages, it has experienced softer demand in many of its products/niches. Even in its most recent performance, the negative impact of softer discretionary spending was evident.

In Q4 2025, its sales amounted to $30.4B, down by 1.5% YoY from $30.9B. This YoY decrease was the same as in my previous coverage, which means that TGT continued to grapple with sales recovery. The percentage decrease may only be a single digit, but $500M was still crucial for the company. The consolation is that this was slightly better than in H1 2025, as sales dropped by 1.9%. This can tell us that TGT was able to cope better with softer market conditions. If you look at its sales per segment, beauty and food and beverage increased, which partially offset the weakness in other product categories. Its advertising segment also remained strong.

We may also infer that TGT reduced its volume in weaker categories so as not to save costs. And I think it was effective, as COGS decreased by 2.0% and completely offset the sales decrease. As a result, the gross profit margin increased to 26.6% versus 26.1% YoY. The problem was that its operating expenses still increased, so the operating margin decreased to 4.5% versus 4.7% YoY. Nonetheless, TGT still derived enough revenue to cover all costs and expenses and remain profitable.

Potential Risks

As a retailer, TGT stays highly sensitive to inflation. And the problem may escalate due to the Middle East tension. Why? Oil prices are already skyrocketing, so fuel prices may most likely follow. This will make the transportation of its products more expensive. Additionally, fuel prices may affect multiple industries, which may exacerbate inflation on top of tariff woes. With that, more consumers may reduce their discretionary spending. Additionally, it may have to raise wages so employees can keep up with the rising prices. With that, inflation may squeeze its sales growth and margins further.

Competition with cheaper alternatives like Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT) must be considered since they can take away a portion of its demand. E-commerce versus driving to TGT stores may offer more convenience and savings. So, this is no longer just a matter of pricing battles but also value propositions.

Potential Risk Mitigants

Before I proceed, I would like to highlight that these are purely risk mitigants, not growth drivers. I believe that these things can help TGT survive these headwinds or cushion their impact. However, these may not be enough to drive its growth and rebound.

One aspect to consider is its business/pricing model. TGT built its own identity through its product lines and pricing. It offers diverse categories that cater mostly to middle-income households. So, its pricing sits in the middle of premium retailers and cheaper alternatives like WMT and dollar shops. It also has its own branding of products to maintain customer trust and loyalty. And I think this continues to work in some ways if you look at its resilient performance in food and beverage, advertising, and beauty. However, it will be challenging for TGT to lower its pricing to attract demand without sacrificing its margins.

Meanwhile, it may derive some benefits from its physical stores. Despite its efforts to adapt to the digital revolution, TGT is still mostly a brick-and-mortar business. This can be capital-intensive and cost-sensitive. Even so, its high concentration may still have some bull cases. For instance, this can serve as a strong asset backing, which can provide some downside protection. In fact, the net value of the PP&E is nearly half of its EV of $70.15B. This can also lead to some monetization flexibility. For instance, TGT can do sale-leasebacks. In short, TGT is not only a retailer but also a real estate business.

Lastly, it still maintains robust liquidity, which assures us of its sustainability and longevity. Its cash and cash equivalents remain high at $5.5B. This should not be surprising since its operating cash inflow is still enough to cover capex. Also, a substantial portion of its operating expenses are noncash items like depreciation, amortization, and share-based compensation. TGT complements it with prudent debt management through timely issuances and repayments. More importantly, it maintains a decent maturity profile. As you can see, only 12% of the total amount will mature within the year. Also, it has more than enough cash to cover it. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.4x, which means that it only needs 1.4 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it has enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $4.56 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.14. The dividend growth is 7.42%, which I derived using the five-year CAGR. Meanwhile, I lowered its risk premium to 6.5% because I realized that 7.0% is already too high and too conservative relative to the 5.0% market standard. Lastly, its Beta is only 1.02, which means that the stock has become less volatile than in my previous coverage. But this is quite higher than 1.00, which means that the range of price swings is still a bit wider than the S&P 500. With all this information, I derived a TP, or target price, of $126.32. This justifies the continued price rally to its current price. But at this level, the gap between the TP and the current price is already narrow. So, upside potential may become limited.

Its price ratios also suggest that TGT is still a bit cheap today. Its P/E shows that the stock trades at only 15.03x its EPS versus the five-year average of 18.09x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $147.10. However, it is important to note that 2022 was an outlier. If we only focus on 2021, 2023, 2024, and 2025, TGT is trading within the range of the previous years. It's not exactly that cheap. Additionally, its EPS growth YoY was negative. If we get the EPS growth using the 2022-2025 CAGR, it will be 8%. However, the PEG is still high at 1.88 (15.03/8), which means that TGT is still fully priced at this level. Hence, I still advise investors to stay on the sidelines.

Technicals

TGT's technicals are still bullish as it moves with higher highs and lows. In my previous coverage, I also failed to note the shifting trend of the 50-day SMA after crossing above the 200-day SMA. This was a crucial aspect since it could have signaled uptrend continuation. That also began when the price crossed above both the 50-day and 200-day SMAs. Now, the stock price is still trending above the two SMAs, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. However, you can also see increasing selling pressures in the past month. It is also worth noting that the market is testing its resistance as the stock trades within $120-124. If the stock price does not break above it, the breakout attempts may weaken. So, it's also important that the stock price does not go below $112-114 for the uptrend to hold. With that, I advise caution at this level.

Its MACD also advises caution as it moves downwards. This signals early bearish divergence, which means that fewer buyers are entering as the stock becomes more expensive. Meanwhile, the histogram is still within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. But note that it is decreasing again close to zero in line with the weakening MACD line. This can tell us that selling volume is starting to catch up again with buying volume. Lastly, RSI in the past two months showed that the stock has become overbought. The trend is also showing a specific range, which also verifies that the market is becoming more cautious at this price.

Conclusion

Target Corporation stays resilient with its prudent cost management, well-diversified business model, and strong liquidity. However, these are not enough to drive further upside, as they are merely risk mitigants amid macroeconomic headwinds. Valuation also appears almost fully priced relative to its dividends and earnings growth, which may limit its upside potential. Technicals are still quite bullish, but recent overbuying may strengthen selling pressures. I'm reiterating my hold rating on this stock.
